parsed.summary: This cumulative diff (v0.4.0→v0.4.6) replaces core infrastructure (process.mjs deleted, retry.mjs and bigmodel-errors.mjs added, glm-client.mjs heavily reworked) across 84 files and 6600+ insertions. The three highest-risk areas are: (1) a fully deleted module with no visible CI gate proving zero dangling imports remain, (2) a new 203-line retry/backoff module wired into the API client without a corresponding integration test, and (3) concurrent-state mutations in tracked-jobs/state that lack crash-safety guards. The happy path likely works; the failure paths under crash, retry, or concurrent access are not adequately defended.

findings.title: Deleted process.mjs — dangling-import crash risk not gated in CI
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs (67 lines) was removed entirely. Every sibling in scripts/lib/ and scripts/glm-companion.mjs was modified in the same diff, which is consistent with import migration — but the only structural guard is scripts/check-imports.mjs, which was itself modified by 5 lines (raising the question of whether the process.mjs reference was simply removed from the allowlist rather than enforced). If any file, test, hook, or dynamically-imported path still references './lib/process.mjs' or '../lib/process.mjs', the application will throw ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND at startup with no fallback. This is a silent-kill defect: it only manifests at runtime import resolution and would not be caught by unit tests that don't exercise that import path.
findings.recommendation: Add a CI gate that runs 'node --check' or a static import tracer (e.g. dependency-cruiser) over every .mjs entry point to prove zero references to the deleted module remain. Alternatively, grep -r "process.mjs" scripts/ and confirm no hits outside check-imports.mjs.

findings.title: New retry module wired into API client without integration test coverage
findings.body: scripts/lib/retry.mjs (203 lines, new) implements exponential backoff with jitter and feeds into glm-client.mjs (155 lines changed). Retry logic is historically a top source of subtle production bugs: off-by-one attempt counting, failure to propagate AbortSignals, non-idempotent request body consumption on replay, and backoff ceiling miscalculation. While tests/retry.test.mjs (352 lines) covers the retry module in isolation, no test file exercises the retry→bigmodel-errors→glm-client pipeline end-to-end. A misclassification in bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 lines, new) could cause the retry layer to either (a) retry a mutating non-idempotent request (data duplication) or (b) give up prematurely on a safely retriable error (silent data loss).
findings.recommendation: Add an integration test that exercises glm-client.mjs through retry.mjs with mocked HTTP responses simulating 429/500/502/503 and verifies: (1) correct attempt count, (2) no double-submission on idempotent vs non-idempotent verbs, (3) AbortSignal cancellation stops retries immediately, (4) bigmodel-errors classification matches retry policy for each error code.

findings.title: State and tracked-jobs files lack crash-safety for concurrent/partial writes
findings.body: scripts/lib/state.mjs (32 lines changed) and scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) manage persistent job state that is read and written during job lifecycle transitions. If the process crashes mid-write (OOM, SIGKILL, disk full) or two invocations run concurrently (--background flag was added per commit d573807), the state file can be left in a corrupted or partially-written state. No file-locking, atomic-rename (write-to-temp-then-rename), or journaling mechanism is visible. A corrupted state file would cause all subsequent glm commands to fail until the user manually deletes the state file — a silent, user-visible outage with no recovery path.
findings.recommendation: Adopt atomic file writes: write to a temp file in the same directory, then fs.rename() to the target path. For concurrent access (--background), add an advisory file lock (proper-lockfile or flock) around state transitions. Add a test that kills the process mid-write and verifies state file integrity on next read.

findings.title: BigModel error dispatch may misclassify unknown error shapes
findings.body: scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 lines, new) classifies API errors to drive retry/backoff decisions. If the upstream API returns an unexpected error shape (new error code, changed response schema, network-level error that doesn't match expected JSON structure), the classifier's default branch determines behavior. A conservative default (don't retry) causes transient failures to surface as permanent errors; a liberal default (always retry) wastes resources and risks amplifying load during outages. Without seeing the default branch, this is a structural risk inherent in any error classifier that becomes the sole decision-maker for retry policy.
findings.recommendation: Ensure the default/unknown-error classification is explicitly fail-safe (retry once with short backoff, then surface the raw error). Add a test case for a completely unrecognised error shape (null body, non-JSON response, unexpected status code) and verify the retry layer handles it without throwing an unhandled exception.

next_steps: Run 'grep -r "process.mjs" scripts/' against the working tree and confirm zero hits outside check-imports.mjs; add this as a CI gate.

Read retry.mjs line-by-line to verify: (a) attempt counter starts at 1 not 0, (b) AbortSignal is checked before each attempt, (c) maxAttempts boundary is '<' not '<=', (d) backoff formula cannot produce NaN or negative delay.
Add an integration test: glm-client → retry → bigmodel-errors with mocked HTTP covering at minimum 429, 502, 503, network timeout, and non-JSON response.
Verify state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs use atomic writes (write-to-temp + rename); if not, implement before release.
Check that --background execution (added in d573807) does not create two concurrent writers to the same state file without locking.
